context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 12th February, 2024
Days Covered: 01 and 02 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)

Please make note of the following instructions:
- Your solutions must be submitted to Gradescope as a single PDF file. While we allow handwritten solutions, we strongly suggest that you typeset in LaTeX, using the template available on Canvas.
- Each problem must be accompanied by a collaboration statement: the students you collaborated with, and any external resources you consulted when solving the problem. Do not leave this blank—instead write "collaborators: none" if appropriate. See the Course Information handout for more details on the collaboration policy.


Problem 1. Collaboration Policy [10 points]

Carefully read the collaboration policy in our Course Information Handout. Then, consider the scenarios described below, and discuss whether and why each one does or does not violate the course homework policies. If the issue is ambiguous, explain how you would resolve it. Simply answering "I'd check with the TA" for each answer is not sufficient.

(a) You host a pset party where ten of you work together to solve the problems before separating to write up your own solutions.
(b) You work with a friend on a problem, taking turns writing ideas and calculations jointly in the same notebook as you work out the solution, then photograph those notes so you can each have a copy of your sketch before you separate to write up your own solutions.
(c) You work together to figure out a detailed solution; one of you then dictates it to the other so that each of you can turn in a solution you've written yourself.
(d) After you've solved a problem, your friend who is still working on the problem asks you for feedback on a solution idea. Leveraging your knowledge of the right answer, you identify and point out a flaw in their proposed solution.
(e) After you've solved a problem, you try to help your friends by acting as a sounding board while they work towards the solution. With the deadline approaching, since they are still stuck, you allow them to skim your writeup to get a general idea of how to approach the problem, which allows them to go off and independently figure out their solution and write it up.
(f) After you've worked with your friend to solve a problem and each done your own writeup, you exchange writeups and examine them to identify and correct any flaws in one or the other.
(g) You remember seeing the homework problem in a problem set of a class you took previously. You look at your notes, see that you got a perfect grade, and copy your solution.
(h) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you write it down that way.
(i) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you explain the solution to your pset collaborators.
(j) While completing a late problem set for partial credit, you read the published solutions as you type your own answers, making sure to adjust the word choice and sentence structure as you go.


Problem 2. Set Formulas and Propositional Formulas [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] Use a truth table to verify that the propositional formula (P AND NOT Q) OR (P AND Q) is equivalent to P.
Note: For propositions, the three notations "NOT Q", "¬Q", and "Q-bar" are interchangeable.

(b) [5 pts] Prove that
    A = (A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B)
for all sets A, B, by showing
    x ∈ A   iff   x ∈ ((A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B))
for all elements x. How does this relate to part (a)?
Note: the set difference A − B is defined on page 105.


Problem 3. Predicate Practice [10 points]

For parts (a)–(d) below, translate the given statements into predicate logic, while preserving the meaning as closely as possible. In addition to boolean logic symbols like AND, OR, NOT, and IMPLIES, and quantifiers ∃ and ∀, you may build predicates using arithmetic, inequalities, and constants.

For this problem, you should use ℕ = {0, 1, 2, ...} as your domain of discourse: all variables are assumed to belong to ℕ, so ∃x and ∀x mean ∃x ∈ ℕ and ∀x ∈ ℕ. Avoid quantifying over other sets, such as "∀p ∈ Primes" or "∃n ≥ 1".

For example, "n is odd" could be translated as
    isOdd(n) := ∃m. (2m + 1 = n)
or, relatedly,
    isNotEven(n) := NOT (∃a. n = 2a).
The predicate "p is a prime number" could be translated to
    isPrime(p) := (p > 1) AND NOT (∃m ∃n. (m > 1 AND n > 1 AND mn = p)).
You may use the predicates isOdd(n) and isPrime(p) by name in your answers below.

(a) [2 pts] The predicate sixish(n) defined as "n is a natural number whose first digit is 6."
    Hint: You may wish to use exponents, e.g., 10^k. Be careful about where you do or don't need quantifiers.
(b) [2 pts] "There are exactly two natural numbers n such that Q(n) is true."
(c) [2 pts] Goldbach's Conjecture: "every even integer greater than 2 can be written as the sum of two primes."
    Note: Do not use quantifier shorthand like "∀n > 2. V(n)". Instead, use the more basic "∀n. W(n)" and include all necessary logic inside W(n).
    Hint: "If n is even and greater than 2, then ...".
(d) [2 pts] "Every prime larger than 100 is sixish."
    Hint: You may refer to "sixish" by name, even if you didn't solve part (a).
(e) [2 pts] Is the predicate R(n) := "∃b. n = 2b + 5" equivalent to isOdd(n)? Prove your answer.
    Hint: Recall that our domain of discourse is ℕ.


Problem 4. Induction and Contradiction [Edited] [20 points]

(a) [10 pts] Suppose Q(x) is a predicate defined for all x ∈ ℝ. Assume Q has the following property: for every x, y ∈ ℝ, if Q(x · y) is true, then at least one of Q(x) or Q(y) must be true.
From this, prove that Q satisfies the following stronger property: for any integer n ≥ 1 and any n numbers x1, x2, ..., xn ∈ ℝ, if Q(x1 · x2 · ... · xn) is true, then at least one of Q(x1), Q(x2), ..., Q(xn) must be true.
Prove the result by induction on n. Be sure to carefully identify your inductive hypothesis P(n), your base case(s), and the assumption and goal in your inductive step.
    Hint: x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1} · xn = (x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1}) · xn

(b) [5 pts] Consider the following theorem:
    Theorem 1. If the product of n ≥ 1 real numbers is irrational, then at least one of those numbers must be irrational.
We will eventually prove this Theorem by citing part (a). In preparation, what should we choose for Q from part (a)? What lemma must we prove about your chosen predicate Q before we're allowed to use part (a)?

(c) [5 pts] Use contradiction to carefully prove the lemma that you identified in part (b), and then finish the proof of Theorem 1.
While proving your lemma, you should not assume any prior knowledge about irrational numbers beyond the definition ("irrational" means "not rational"), but you may use familiar facts about rational numbers.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of overwhelmed by this pset already. Could we start with Problem 1? I read the policy stuff, but the scenarios all blur together and I can’t tell what actually counts as allowed vs not allowed.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good place to start. Before we look at any specific scenario, let me ask you this: after reading the policy, what would you say are the two or three most important things the policy is trying to protect or ensure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed and wants to start with Problem 1. This is a reasonable starting point. Rather than walking them through each scenario, I should help them build a framework for evaluating scenarios themselves using the Socratic method. I'll ask them to identify the key principles from the collaboration policy first, so they can apply those principles independently to each scenario. This is guided discovery - let them extract the rules before applying them.

exchanges.student: I guess it wants people to learn the material themselves, and also not just copy? But I’m not sure how that turns into answers for all the little cases, because some of them feel basically the same to me.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts. Now look back at the policy text itself - it actually draws a pretty explicit line between two phases of doing a problem set. Can you find and name those two phases? That distinction is the key tool for analyzing most of the scenarios.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two reasonable core principles but hasn't been precise enough to distinguish between the cases. I should push them to be more precise about the distinction the policy draws - specifically the solving vs. writing distinction, which is the key framework the policy explicitly lays out. This will give them a tool to analyze each scenario. I'll ask a targeted question to help them find this distinction in the policy text.

exchanges.student: I think it was something like solving versus writing? I saw that, but then I got lost because people can talk during solving, so I can’t tell when shared work turns into not-okay writing help.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right - solving vs. writing. So now tell me: according to the policy, what is specifically allowed during the solving phase, and what is the key requirement for the writing phase?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the solving vs. writing distinction. Now I should help them get more precise about what each phase actually allows, rather than giving them a summary. I'll ask them what the policy specifically says about each phase, pushing them to extract the concrete rules themselves.

exchanges.student: During solving I think I’m allowed to collaborate in a small group and work out ideas together. For writing, it has to be entirely my own words and I’m not supposed to look at other people’s writeups or basically translate shared notes too directly. But then stuff like shared scratch work or giving feedback still confuses me.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the framework down well. Let's apply it directly - take scenario (b), where two students share a notebook and photograph the notes. Using what you just said about the two phases, where do you think that scenario stands?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid grasp of the two phases now. Rather than continuing to discuss the framework abstractly, I should push them to apply it to actual scenarios. They've been circling the same conceptual territory for a few exchanges - it's time to move to application. I'll pick one of the confusing cases they mentioned (shared scratch work) and ask them to apply the framework directly to a specific scenario from the problem set.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m stuck, because it sounds like solving together, which seems allowed, but photographing the notes makes it feel like they’re carrying shared material into the writeup. I can kind of argue both sides and I don’t know which detail matters most.
exchanges.tutor: Good - you're identifying real tension. Here's a focusing question: the policy says something specific about consulting shared notes during the writing phase. Does photographing the notes make it more or less likely that they'll consult those notes while writing up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely wrestling with the ambiguity here, which is good engagement. Rather than resolving it for them, I should help them think about what the *purpose* of the photography is - is it helping them remember their own thinking, or is it substituting for their own writeup? The policy specifically warns against directly consulting shared notes when writing. I'll ask a targeted question to help them see which element is decisive.

exchanges.student: Yeah, more likely. So I guess that probably makes it a violation or at least really risky? I’m still fuzzy on whether the problem is the photo itself or the using-it-while-writing part, and I feel like if we do one case at a time I’m going to miss the overall pattern.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with (b) - the problem isn't the photo itself, it's what it enables. Now you actually have everything you need to work through the rest. Try grouping the scenarios yourself: which ones are clearly fine, which clearly violate, and which are genuinely ambiguous? That's the pattern you're looking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress on (b) and has correctly identified that the photo creates risk of consulting shared notes during writing. They're also showing metacognitive awareness about wanting to see the pattern across scenarios. I should validate their conclusion on (b) and then encourage them to use the framework they've built to tackle the remaining scenarios themselves, rather than going case by case with me. This respects the solving vs. writing framework they've internalized and pushes them toward independent application.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t see the grouping yet. Could you maybe just give me the overall pattern across all the scenarios, because right now each one feels like a tiny exception and I’m not seeing the rule.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the pattern directly - that's for you to discover. But let's build it from the ground up. Try scenario (c): one person dictates a solution so both can write it in their own hand. Which phase does that touch, solving or writing? What does the policy say about that specific situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer/pattern directly, which I should refuse. However, they're expressing genuine frustration. Rather than giving the pattern, I should redirect them to try applying the framework to just one more concrete scenario - a clearer one - so they can build confidence and start seeing the pattern emerge themselves. I'll pick a simpler scenario like (g) or (c) which is more clear-cut, to help them build momentum.

exchanges.student: I think that touches writing, because even if they physically write it themselves, it’s basically the same wording/structure being fed to them. I’m still feeling like I need a bigger picture summary though, because I’m worried I’ll classify one or two right and then mess up the rest for inconsistent reasons.
exchanges.tutor: Notice what just happened: you correctly analyzed (b) and (c) using the same framework. That consistency IS the pattern. Also - the problem itself acknowledges some cases are ambiguous. You don't need a perfect rule that handles every case cleanly. Try (e) and (f) on your own now and tell me what you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly analyzed (c) and is showing good reasoning. However, they keep asking for a big picture summary, which would essentially be giving them the answer. They've now correctly analyzed (b) and (c) using the framework. I should point out that they ARE building the pattern themselves through these cases, and encourage them to trust the framework they already have. I should also note that some scenarios are genuinely ambiguous by design - the assignment even says 'if the issue is ambiguous, explain how you would resolve it.' I'll push them to try a few more on their own now.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure. (e) feels bad because they skim a writeup, but it says just to get the general idea, so I don’t know if that changes it. And (f) feels maybe okay because it’s after both people already wrote their own stuff, but also maybe not okay because they’re still looking at each other’s writeups.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts on both. For (e), the policy says something explicit about showing writeups - does it make any exception for 'just a general idea'? Check the exact wording and tell me what you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making reasonable progress on both (e) and (f). Their instincts are pointing in the right direction on both. For (e), they correctly identify that skimming a writeup is problematic but are unsure if 'general idea' is a mitigating factor - I should help them check that against what the policy explicitly says. For (f), they're also close but need to check the policy text more carefully. Rather than confirming or denying, I'll ask them to check their reasoning against specific policy language.

exchanges.student: I think it says I shouldn’t show my writeup to peers and shouldn’t look at theirs, period. So “just skimming” probably still violates it. I’m still kind of drowning in this though — could you just explain how you’d think about all of Problem 1 at a high level?
exchanges.tutor: You've now correctly worked through (b), (c), and (e) yourself - that's not drowning, that's progress. At this point, I'd encourage you to start drafting your written answers for the cases you've analyzed. That will help you see where your reasoning is solid and where gaps remain. What does your draft for (e) look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly analyzed (e) using the policy text. However, they're again asking me to give them a high-level summary of the whole problem, which I should refuse. They've now successfully analyzed (b), (c), and (e) on their own using the framework. I should point this out and push them toward attempting a written draft of their answers rather than continuing to discuss cases one by one. We've had multiple exchanges now and they have the tools - it's time to ask them to integrate what they've learned into actual written responses.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently applied Socratic method throughout the conversation, guiding the student to derive the solving/writing distinction themselves and then apply it case by case. No direct answers or near-submission-ready content was provided. The tutor built on student progress at each turn, acknowledged correct reasoning, and redirected requests for a 'big picture summary' without giving it away. Responses were appropriately concise and supportive. No ambiguous cases required smaller deductions.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student the answer or pattern directly, even when explicitly asked multiple times. Each turn posed a focused question that led the student to reason through the scenario themselves. No submission-ready content was produced. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student reasoning (e.g., Turn 8 noting the student had correctly analyzed (b) and (c)), and used diagnostic questioning before addressing confusion. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, pointing the student to exact policy wording and noting when their reasoning method was sound. 2.1: The tutor advanced the conversation each turn rather than cycling; when the student asked for a summary, the tutor redirected to a new scenario rather than repeating the same move. 2.2: The conversation stayed anchored to Problem 1 throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-targeted, and maintained a supportive, coaching tone without implying grades.
